examples.explanation: Total sum = 20, target = 5. Sorted: [5,4,3,3,2,2,1]. Backtrack: assign 5 to subset0 (sum=5), 4 to subset1 (sum=4), 3 to subset1 (sum=7 >5, prune), try subset2 (sum=3), 3 to subset2 (sum=6 >5, prune), try subset3 (sum=3), 2 to subset1 (sum=6 >5, prune), try subset2 (sum=5), 2 to subset3 (sum=5), 1 to subset0 (sum=6 >5, backtrack), reassign 1 to subset1 (sum=5), all subsets sum=5, count=1. Continue exploring all assignments: total valid partitions = 4.

Total sum = 10, target = 5. Sorted: [4,3,2,1]. Backtrack: assign 4 to subset0 (sum=4), 3 to subset0 (sum=7 >5, prune), try subset1 (sum=3), 2 to subset0 (sum=6 >5, prune), try subset1 (sum=5), 1 to subset0 (sum=5), all subsets sum=5, count=1. Another path: assign 4 to subset0, 3 to subset1, 2 to subset0 (sum=6 >5, prune), try subset1 (sum=5), 1 to subset0 (sum=5), count=2. Total = 2.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Backtracking O(k^n)
approaches.summary: Use backtracking to assign each element to one of k partitions, counting valid partitions where each subset has equal sum. This explores all possible assignments but is inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count K-Partitions with Equal Sum via Backtracking
ladders.desc: Complete backtracking solution to count ways to partition array into k subsets with equal sum.
ladders.explanation: This is the full implementation of the brute-force backtracking approach for the K-partition problem. The intuition is to explore all possible assignments of each array element to one of k subsets, while ensuring each subset's sum equals the target sum (total sum divided by k). We use backtracking to systematically try assignments, pruning invalid branches early when a subset exceeds the target sum. The algorithm starts by checking if the total sum is divisible by k; if not, return 0. Then, sort the array in descending order to place larger elements first, which helps prune invalid branches earlier. We maintain an array 'subsetSums' to track the current sum of each subset. The recursive function 'backtrack(index)' tries to assign the element at 'index' to each subset that hasn't exceeded the target sum. If all elements are assigned (index == n), we check if all subsets have exactly the target sum and increment the count. The time complexity is O(k^n) in the worst case, as each element can go into any of k subsets, but pruning reduces this in practice. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth and the subsetSums array. This approach is foundational but inefficient for large n or k, serving as a baseline for understanding more optimized DP solutions. Common mistakes include not pruning early (leading to exponential blow-up), forgetting to handle the case where total sum isn't divisible by k, and not resetting state during backtracking. The connection to L1 is that this level integrates the backtracking subroutine with overall problem setup and validation.

examples.trace: Total sum = 20, target = 5. Sorted: [5,4,3,3,2,2,1]. Backtrack: assign 5 to subset0 (sum=5), 4 to subset1 (sum=4), 3 to subset1 (sum=7 >5, prune), try subset2 (sum=3), 3 to subset2 (sum=6 >5, prune), try subset3 (sum=3), 2 to subset1 (sum=6 >5, prune), try subset2 (sum=5), 2 to subset3 (sum=5), 1 to subset0 (sum=6 >5, backtrack), reassign 1 to subset1 (sum=5), all subsets sum=5, count=1. Continue exploring all assignments: total valid partitions = 4.

Total sum = 10, target = 5. Sorted: [4,3,2,1]. Backtrack: assign 4 to subset0 (sum=4), 3 to subset0 (sum=7 >5, prune), try subset1 (sum=3), 2 to subset0 (sum=6 >5, prune), try subset1 (sum=5), 1 to subset0 (sum=5), all subsets sum=5, count=1. Another path: assign 4 to subset0, 3 to subset1, 2 to subset0 (sum=6 >5, prune), try subset1 (sum=5), 1 to subset0 (sum=5), count=2. Total = 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking Assignment of Elements to Subsets
ladders.desc: Recursive function to assign array elements to k subsets while tracking sums.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core backtracking function that assigns each element from the array to one of k subsets. The intuition is to treat each subset as a 'bucket' and try placing the current element into each bucket that hasn't exceeded the target sum. We use recursion to explore all possibilities: at each step (for element at index 'i'), we iterate through all k subsets and try adding the element to subset 'j' if adding it doesn't exceed the target sum. If it fits, we update subsetSums[j], recurse to the next element (i+1), and then backtrack by subtracting the element from subsetSums[j]. When we reach the last element (i == n), we check if all subsets have exactly the target sum and increment a global count. The time complexity for this subroutine is O(k^n) in worst case, but pruning when subset sum exceeds target reduces branches. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack. This feeds into L0 by being the main recursive engine; L0 adds the setup (checking divisibility, sorting) and calls this function. Common mistakes include not pruning (causing unnecessary recursion), not resetting state after recursion (leading to incorrect sums), and using a global count without proper management. The pseudocode: def backtrack(i): if i == n: if all subsetSums == target: count += 1; return; for j in range(k): if subsetSums[j] + nums[i] <= target: subsetSums[j] += nums[i]; backtrack(i+1); subsetSums[j] -= nums[i]; if subsetSums[j] == 0: break; // optimization to avoid duplicate subsets.

examples.trace: Start: i=0, nums[0]=2. Try subset0: subsetSums=[2,0] (<=3), recurse i=1. i=1, nums[1]=2. Try subset0: subsetSums=[4,0] (>3, prune), try subset1: subsetSums=[2,2], recurse i=2. i=2, nums[2]=2. Try subset0: subsetSums=[4,2] (>3, prune), try subset1: subsetSums=[2,4] (>3, prune), backtrack. i=1 backtrack, try subset1 first: subsetSums=[0,2], recurse i=2. i=2, try subset0: subsetSums=[2,2], recurse i=3. i=3 (n=3): check subsetSums=[2,2] != target 3, no count. End.

Start: i=0, nums[0]=1. Try subset0: subsetSums=[1,0,0], recurse i=1. i=1, try subset0: subsetSums=[2,0,0] (>1, prune), try subset1: subsetSums=[1,1,0], recurse i=2. i=2, try subset0: subsetSums=[2,1,0] (>1, prune), try subset1: subsetSums=[1,2,0] (>1, prune), try subset2: subsetSums=[1,1,1], recurse i=3. i=3: all sums=1, count=1. Backtrack and explore all permutations: total 6 ways.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pruning Invalid Branches in Backtracking
ladders.desc: Technique to stop recursion early when a subset sum exceeds target.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the pruning logic that makes backtracking feasible by avoiding unnecessary recursive calls. The intuition is that if adding the current element to a subset causes its sum to exceed the target sum, there's no point in continuing down that path, as it can never lead to a valid partition. During the backtracking process, before assigning an element to a subset, we check if 'subsetSums[j] + nums[i] <= target'. If this condition is false, we skip that subset and move to the next one. This simple check dramatically reduces the number of recursive calls, especially when elements are large relative to the target. Additionally, an optimization is to break out of the loop if 'subsetSums[j] == 0' after backtracking, because assigning to an empty subset first avoids duplicate permutations of subsets (since subsets are indistinguishable). The time complexity improvement is data-dependent; in worst case, it's still O(k^n), but with pruning, it's much faster in practice. Space complexity remains O(n). This feeds into L1 as the conditional check within the backtracking loop. Common mistakes include incorrect inequality (using < instead of <=), not applying the optimization for empty subsets, and pruning too aggressively (e.g., pruning when sum equals target but more elements remain). The pseudocode snippet: for j in range(k): if subsetSums[j] + nums[i] <= target: // assign and recurse; if subsetSums[j] == 0: break; // after backtracking.

examples.trace: Start: i=0, nums[0]=5. Try subset0: subsetSums[0]+5=5 >3, prune, skip subset0. Try subset1: subsetSums[1]+5=5 >3, prune, skip subset1. No valid assignment, backtrack ends early without recursing to i=1.

Start: i=0, nums[0]=2. Try subset0: subsetSums=[2,0] (<=2.5), recurse i=1. i=1, nums[1]=2. Try subset0: subsetSums=[4,0] (>2.5, prune), try subset1: subsetSums=[2,2] (<=2.5), recurse i=2. i=2, nums[2]=1. Try subset0: subsetSums=[3,2] (>2.5, prune), try subset1: subsetSums=[2,3] (>2.5, prune), backtrack. Without pruning, would try more invalid paths.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Tree Traversal for Combinatorial Assignments
ladders.desc: Using recursion to explore all ways to assign items to groups.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental recursive pattern for exploring combinatorial assignments, which is essential for backtracking. The intuition is to model the problem as a tree where each level corresponds to an element in the array, and each branch represents assigning that element to one of k subsets. Recursion allows us to traverse this tree depth-first: at each node (element), we try all possible branches (subsets), recurse to the next element, and backtrack to try other branches. The base case is when we've processed all elements (reached a leaf), where we check if the assignment is valid. This approach systematically enumerates all k^n possible assignments. The time complexity is O(k^n) due to the branching factor k and depth n. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion call stack. This feeds into L2 by providing the recursive framework; L2 adds pruning within this traversal. Common mistakes include infinite recursion (missing base case), not backtracking properly (failing to undo changes), and incorrect state management across recursive calls. The pseudocode: def explore(i): if i == n: // base case: check validity; return; for j in range(k): // try assigning element i to subset j; update state; explore(i+1); revert state; // backtrack.

examples.trace: Root: i=0 (A). Branch0: assign A to subset0, state: subset0=1. Recurse i=1 (B). Branch0: assign B to subset0, state: subset0=3, recurse i=2 (base case). Branch1: assign B to subset1, state: subset0=1, subset1=2, recurse i=2. Backtrack to i=0. Branch1: assign A to subset1, state: subset1=1. Recurse i=1. Branch0: assign B to subset0, state: subset0=2, subset1=1, recurse i=2. Branch1: assign B to subset1, state: subset1=3, recurse i=2. Total 4 leaves explored.

Root: i=0 (X). Branch0: assign X to subset0, state: subset0=5, recurse i=1 (base case). Branch1: assign X to subset1, state: subset1=5, recurse i=1. Branch2: assign X to subset2, state: subset2=5, recurse i=1. Total 3 leaves explored.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Maintaining and Updating Subset Sums Array
ladders.desc: Tracking cumulative sums of elements assigned to each subset.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the basic operation of maintaining an array 'subsetSums' that stores the current total sum of elements assigned to each of the k subsets. The intuition is that as we assign elements to subsets during backtracking, we need to keep track of how 'full' each subset is to enforce the constraint that no subset exceeds the target sum. The array is initialized with all zeros. When we assign an element 'nums[i]' to subset 'j', we add 'nums[i]' to 'subsetSums[j]'. After exploring that assignment recursively, we subtract 'nums[i]' from 'subsetSums[j]' to backtrack and restore the state for trying other assignments. This operation is O(1) per update. The space complexity is O(k) for the array. This feeds into L3 as the state management within recursive traversal; L3 uses this array to track assignments. Common mistakes include not initializing to zero, forgetting to subtract during backtracking (leading to incorrect sums), and using a single variable instead of an array for multiple subsets. The pseudocode: subsetSums = [0]*k; // initialize; subsetSums[j] += nums[i]; // assign; subsetSums[j] -= nums[i]; // backtrack.

examples.input: k=3, initial subsetSums=[0,0,0], assign element 5 to subset1
examples.trace: Start: subsetSums = [0,0,0]. Operation: subsetSums[1] += 5. Result: subsetSums = [0,5,0]. To backtrack: subsetSums[1] -= 5. Result: subsetSums = [0,0,0].

examples.input: k=2, subsetSums=[3,2], assign element 4 to subset0
examples.trace: Start: subsetSums = [3,2]. Check: 3+4=7. If target=5, 7>5, prune and do not update. If target=10, update: subsetSums[0] += 4, result [7,2]. Backtrack: subsetSums[0] -= 4, result [3,2].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Summation of Array Elements
ladders.desc: Calculating the total sum of elements in an array.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: computing the sum of all elements in an array. The intuition is that for the K-partition problem, we need to know the total sum to determine if it's divisible by k and to calculate the target sum per subset (total sum / k). The operation involves iterating through each element in the array and adding its value to a running total. For an array 'nums' of length n, we initialize a variable 'total' to 0, then loop from i=0 to n-1 and do 'total += nums[i]'. The time complexity is O(n), and space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L4 and higher levels as the initial step; without the total sum, we cannot set up the backtracking problem. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loops, using the wrong variable type (e.g., integer overflow for large sums), and not handling empty arrays. The pseudocode: total = 0; for num in nums: total += num; return total.

examples.trace: Start: total=0. Add nums[0]=1: total=1. Add nums[1]=2: total=3. Add nums[2]=3: total=6. Add nums[3]=4: total=10. Return 10.
Start: total=0. Add 5: total=5. Add -2: total=3. Add 3: total=6. Add 0: total=6. Return 6.

approaches.name: Approach 2: DP with Bitmask and Subset Sum O(n * 2^n)
approaches.summary: Use dynamic programming with bitmask states to represent subsets, counting ways to partition the set into k subsets of equal sum. This leverages subset sum DP to avoid redundant calculations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Counting K-Partitions with Bitmask DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP with bitmask states to count ways to partition a set into k subsets of equal sum.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting the number of ways to partition an array of integers into k subsets such that each subset has the same sum. The DP with bitmask approach efficiently handles this by representing subsets as bitmasks and using dynamic programming to count valid partitions. The intuition is to treat each subset as a 'bucket' that must be filled to the target sum (total_sum/k). We use DP[mask] to store the number of ways to achieve the current subset sum configuration represented by mask. For each mask, we try adding elements to the current subset until it reaches the target sum, then move to the next subset. The key insight is that we only need to track which elements are used (via mask) and the current subset sum modulo target, reducing state space. The algorithm: 1. Calculate total sum and target sum per subset (must be divisible by k). 2. Initialize DP[0] = 1. 3. For each mask from 0 to (1<<n)-1, if DP[mask] > 0, compute current subset sum from mask. 4. For each unused element i, if adding it doesn't exceed target, update DP[mask|(1<<i)] += DP[mask]. 5. If current subset sum equals target, we can start a new subset by resetting the sum tracking. 6. The answer is DP[(1<<n)-1] divided by k! to account for subset ordering. Time: O(n * 2^n) for iterating masks and elements. Space: O(2^n) for DP array. Connection to L1: This integrates the subset sum counting subroutine to build complete partitions. Common mistakes: Not handling subset ordering (dividing by k!), missing the modulo target reset, or incorrect bitmask updates. Real code pattern: dp = [0]*(1<<n); dp[0]=1; target = total//k; for mask in range(1<<n): if dp[mask]==0: continue; cur_sum = sum(nums[i] for i in range(n) if mask>>i&1) % target; for i in range(n): if not (mask>>i&1) and cur_sum+nums[i]<=target: dp[mask|(1<<i)] += dp[mask]; return dp[-1]//factorial(k);

examples.trace: Total sum = 10, target = 5. DP[0]=1. Mask 0: cur_sum=0. Add 1: mask=0001, dp=1, cur_sum=1. Add 2: mask=0010, dp=1, cur_sum=2. Add 3: mask=0100, dp=1, cur_sum=3. Add 4: mask=1000, dp=1, cur_sum=4. Mask 0001: cur_sum=1. Add 4: mask=1001, dp=1, cur_sum=5 (target reached, reset cur_sum=0). Mask 0010: cur_sum=2. Add 3: mask=0110, dp=1, cur_sum=5 (reset). Mask 0100: cur_sum=3. Add 2: mask=0110, dp becomes 2 (from 0010 and 0100), cur_sum=5 (reset). Continue processing all masks. Final mask 1111 has dp=4. Divide by 2! = 2, answer = 2. Valid partitions: [1,4],[2,3] and [2,3],[1,4] (considered same after division).

Total sum = 12, target = 4. All elements are 2. DP[0]=1. Process masks: Each step adds a 2 to cur_sum. When cur_sum reaches 4 (after two 2's), reset. The DP counts all ways to group six 2's into three groups of two. Total ways to choose 2 out of 6 for first group: C(6,2)=15, then choose 2 out of 4 for second: C(4,2)=6, last group fixed: 1. Total raw dp = 15*6*1=90. Divide by 3! = 6, answer = 15. Step-by-step: Mask progression builds combinations like 001111 (first group elements 0,1), etc.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Counting Subset Sums with Bitmask DP
ladders.desc: DP function to count ways to achieve target sum using subsets of elements.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine counts the number of ways to select subsets from an array that sum to a given target, using bitmask DP. The intuition is to represent each subset as a bitmask where bit i is 1 if element i is included. We use DP[mask] to store the number of ways to achieve the sum represented by mask. By iterating over all masks and adding unused elements, we build up counts for all possible subsets. This is efficient because it avoids recomputing sums for overlapping subsets. The algorithm: 1. Initialize DP[0] = 1 (empty subset sums to 0). 2. For mask from 0 to (1<<n)-1, if DP[mask] > 0, compute current sum from mask. 3. For each element i not in mask, if current sum + nums[i] <= target, update DP[mask | (1<<i)] += DP[mask]. 4. The result is the sum of DP[mask] for all masks where sum equals target. Time: O(n * 2^n) as we iterate over n elements for each of 2^n masks. Space: O(2^n) for DP array. Connection to L0: This subroutine is used repeatedly in the full problem to fill each subset to the target sum. Common mistakes: Not initializing DP[0]=1, incorrect bitmask operations, or missing the <= target condition. Real code pattern: dp = [0]*(1<<n); dp[0]=1; target = given; for mask in range(1<<n): if dp[mask]==0: continue; cur = sum(nums[i] for i if mask>>i&1); for i in range(n): if not (mask>>i&1) and cur+nums[i]<=target: dp[mask|(1<<i)] += dp[mask]; result = sum(dp[m] for m in range(1<<n) if sum(nums[i] for i if m>>i&1)==target);

examples.trace: DP[0]=1. Mask 0: cur=0. Add 1: mask=001, dp=1, cur=1. Add 2: mask=010, dp=1, cur=2. Add 3: mask=100, dp=1, cur=3 (target reached). Mask 001: cur=1. Add 2: mask=011, dp=1, cur=3 (target). Add 3: mask=101, dp=1, cur=4 (>target, skip). Mask 010: cur=2. Add 1: mask=011, dp becomes 2 (from 001 and 010), cur=3 (target). Add 3: mask=110, dp=1, cur=5 (>target). Mask 100: cur=3 (target). No adds. Mask 011: cur=3 (target). No adds. Masks with sum=3: 100, 011. dp[100]=1, dp[011]=2, total=2. Ways: {3} and {1,2}.

DP[0]=1. Mask 0: cur=0. Add first 2: mask=001, dp=1, cur=2. Add second 2: mask=010, dp=1, cur=2. Add third 2: mask=100, dp=1, cur=2. Mask 001: cur=2. Add second 2: mask=011, dp=1, cur=4 (target). Add third 2: mask=101, dp=1, cur=4 (target). Mask 010: cur=2. Add first 2: mask=011, dp becomes 2, cur=4. Add third 2: mask=110, dp=1, cur=4. Mask 100: cur=2. Add first 2: mask=101, dp becomes 2, cur=4. Add second 2: mask=110, dp becomes 2, cur=4. Masks with sum=4: 011, 101, 110. dp[011]=2, dp[101]=2, dp[110]=2, total=6? Wait, careful: dp[011] gets contributions from mask 001 adding element 1 (index 1) and mask 010 adding element 0. So dp[011]=2. Similarly others. But each mask represents a unique subset? Yes, but subsets are same elements? For [2,2,2], subsets of sum 4 are any two elements. There are C(3,2)=3 ways. But dp counts 6 because it distinguishes order of addition. So we need to divide by factorial? No, in this subroutine, we count distinct subsets as bitmasks, which are unique. But with identical elements, bitmasks are distinct indices. So 3 subsets: indices (0,1), (0,2), (1,2). Corresponding masks: 011, 101, 110. Each has dp=2? Let's recompute: Start dp[0]=1. Mask 0: add element 0 -> mask 001 dp=1; add element 1 -> mask 010 dp=1; add element 2 -> mask 100 dp=1. Mask 001 (cur=2): add element 1 -> mask 011 dp=1; add element 2 -> mask 101 dp=1. Mask 010 (cur=2): add element 0 -> mask 011 dp becomes 2; add element 2 -> mask 110 dp=1. Mask 100 (cur=2): add element 0 -> mask 101 dp becomes 2; add element 1 -> mask 110 dp becomes 2. So dp[011]=2, dp[101]=2, dp[110]=2. Sum for sum=4: 2+2+2=6. But actual subsets: 3. The discrepancy is because dp counts ways to build each subset, and with identical elements, different orders give same subset but different dp paths. So for counting subsets, we should take unique masks, not sum dp. Thus result = number of masks with sum=target = 3. So output 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bitmask Representation of Subsets
ladders.desc: Using bitmasks to efficiently represent and manipulate subsets in DP.
ladders.explanation: Bitmask representation is a technique where we use an integer's binary bits to represent a subset of elements from a set of size n. Each bit corresponds to an element: bit i is 1 if element i is in the subset, 0 otherwise. This allows efficient subset iteration and manipulation using bitwise operations. The intuition is that integers from 0 to 2^n - 1 naturally enumerate all possible subsets, enabling DP over subsets with O(2^n) states. Key operations: checking if element i is in mask: (mask >> i) & 1; adding element i: mask | (1 << i); iterating over all masks: for mask in range(1<<n). This is efficient because bitwise operations are O(1), and we can compute subset sums dynamically by tracking which bits are set. In the context of k-partition, we use mask to represent which elements have been assigned to subsets so far. The DP state tracks the number of ways to achieve a particular mask configuration, and we transition by adding unused elements to the current subset. Time: Iterating over 2^n masks is exponential but manageable for n up to ~20. Space: O(2^n) for storing DP values. Connection to L1: This representation is used in the subset sum counting subroutine to iterate over subsets. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in bit shifts, confusing mask indices with element values, or not handling large n where 2^n is intractable. Real code pattern: n = len(nums); for mask in range(1<<n): subset = [nums[i] for i in range(n) if (mask>>i)&1]; sum_mask = sum(subset); # Or compute incrementally.

examples.input: nums = [5,10,15], list all subsets via bitmasks
examples.trace: n=3, masks 0 to 7. Mask 0 (000): no bits set, subset []. Mask 1 (001): bit 0 set, subset [5]. Mask 2 (010): bit 1 set, subset [10]. Mask 3 (011): bits 0 and 1 set, subset [5,10]. Mask 4 (100): bit 2 set, subset [15]. Mask 5 (101): bits 0 and 2 set, subset [5,15]. Mask 6 (110): bits 1 and 2 set, subset [10,15]. Mask 7 (111): all bits set, subset [5,10,15]. Sums: 0,5,10,15,15,20,25,30.

Mask 5 in binary: 101. Bits: position 0 is 1 (include nums[0]=1), position 1 is 0 (exclude nums[1]=2), position 2 is 1 (include nums[2]=3). Sum = 1 + 3 = 4. Step-by-step: Check bit 0: (5>>0)&1 = 1&1=1, add 1. Bit 1: (5>>1)&1 = 2&1=0, skip. Bit 2: (5>>2)&1 = 1&1=1, add 3. Total 4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Dynamic Programming State Transitions
ladders.desc: Understanding how DP states are defined and transitioned using previous results.
ladders.explanation: Dynamic programming (DP) is a method to solve problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results to avoid recomputation. In this context, we define a state that represents a partial solution, and a transition rule that builds new states from existing ones. For subset problems, a common state is DP[mask] = number of ways to achieve the subset represented by mask. The intuition is that we can build larger subsets by adding elements to smaller subsets, and the count for a new mask is the sum of counts from masks that can reach it by adding one element. This works because the order of adding elements doesn't matter for the final subset, so we can iterate masks in increasing order of bits set. The algorithm: Initialize DP[0] = 1. For each mask, if DP[mask] > 0, for each element i not in mask, new_mask = mask | (1<<i); DP[new_mask] += DP[mask]. This ensures each subset is counted exactly once per ordering? Actually, it counts each ordering separately, which we adjust later. Time: O(n * 2^n) as each mask considers up to n transitions. Space: O(2^n). Connection to L2: This DP transition uses bitmask representation to efficiently compute state updates. Common mistakes: Not initializing base case, updating DP in wrong order (should iterate masks ascending), or double-counting due to unordered transitions. Real code pattern: dp = [0]*(1<<n); dp[0]=1; for mask in range(1<<n): if dp[mask]: for i in range(n): if not (mask>>i&1): new = mask|(1<<i); dp[new] += dp[mask];

examples.input: nums = [a,b,c] (values irrelevant for state transitions), trace DP fills

examples.trace: n=2, dp size 4. dp[0]=1. Mask 0: add element 0 → mask 01, dp[01]=1; add element 1 → mask 10, dp[10]=1. Mask 01: add element 1 → mask 11, dp[11]=1. Mask 10: add element 0 → mask 11, dp[11]+=1 → dp[11]=2. Final: dp[00]=1, dp[01]=1, dp[10]=1, dp[11]=2.

testCases.input: n=5, number of masks with dp=1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bitwise Manipulations for Subsets
ladders.desc: Fundamental bitwise operations to check, set, and iterate bits in integers.
ladders.explanation: Bitwise operations are low-level instructions that manipulate individual bits of integers. For subset representation, we use these operations to efficiently handle membership and updates. Key operations: 1. Left shift (<<): 1 << i creates a mask with only bit i set (e.g., 1<<2 = 4 binary 100). 2. Bitwise OR (|): mask | (1<<i) sets bit i to 1 in mask, adding element i. 3. Bitwise AND (&): mask & (1<<i) checks if bit i is set; if non-zero, element i is in the subset. Often used as (mask >> i) & 1 to get a 0/1 value. 4. Iteration: for mask in range(1<<n) loops over all subsets. The intuition is that integers are stored in binary, so these operations are O(1) and allow compact subset handling. For example, to check if element 3 is in a subset: if mask & (1<<3): ... To add element 3: new_mask = mask | (1<<3). To remove element 3: new_mask = mask & ~(1<<3). This is efficient compared to using sets or lists. Connection to L3: These operations enable the DP state transitions on bitmask states. Common mistakes: Using ^ for OR, off-by-one in shifts (1<<0 = 1, not 0), or not using parentheses due to operator precedence. Real code pattern: n = 5; mask = 0; mask = mask | (1<<2) # adds element 2; if mask & (1<<2): print('element 2 present'); for i in range(n): if (mask>>i)&1: # element i is in mask.

examples.input: n=4, perform: start mask=0, add elements 1 and 3, check if 2 is present, remove 1
examples.trace: Start mask=0 (0000). Add element 1: mask = 0 | (1<<1) = 0 | 2 = 2 (0010). Add element 3: mask = 2 | (1<<3) = 2 | 8 = 10 (1010). Check if element 2 is present: (10 >> 2) & 1 = (2) & 1 = 0, so false. Remove element 1: mask = 10 & ~(1<<1) = 10 & ~2 = 10 & 13 = 8 (1000). Binary: 1010 & 1101 = 1000. Final mask=8.

examples.input: n=3, iterate all masks and list which elements are set
examples.trace: n=3, masks 0 to 7. Mask 0 (000): no elements. Mask 1 (001): element 0. Mask 2 (010): element 1. Mask 3 (011): elements 0 and 1. Mask 4 (100): element 2. Mask 5 (101): elements 0 and 2. Mask 6 (110): elements 1 and 2. Mask 7 (111): all elements. Use loop: for mask in range(8): elements = [i for i in range(3) if (mask>>i)&1].

testCases.input: n=3, mask that has bits 0 and 2 set
n=4, mask with all bits set
n=4, mask with no bits set

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Numbers and Bits
ladders.desc: Understanding binary representation and bits as fundamental units.
ladders.explanation: Binary numbers are base-2 numerals that use only two digits: 0 and 1. Each digit is called a bit. A bit is the smallest unit of data, representing a state like on/off or true/false. In computing, integers are stored in binary format. For example, decimal 5 is binary 101 (1*4 + 0*2 + 1*1). The position of a bit (from right, starting at 0) indicates its value as a power of 2: bit i has value 2^i. This is crucial for bitmask techniques because we can use each bit to represent the presence or absence of an element in a set. If we have n elements, we need n bits, so we use integers up to 2^n - 1. For instance, with 3 elements, we use 3-bit numbers from 0 (000) to 7 (111). Bit manipulation operations like AND, OR, and shift work directly on these binary representations. The intuition is that sets can be mapped to binary strings naturally, enabling efficient storage and operations. Connection to L4: This foundation allows us to use bitwise operations for subset handling. Common mistakes: Confusing binary with decimal, not understanding bit positions, or thinking bits start from 1. Real code pattern: In Python, bin(5) returns '0b101'; 5 >> 1 shifts bits right to get 2 (binary 10); 5 & 3 performs bitwise AND.

examples.input: Convert decimal 13 to binary, list bits from position 0 to 3
examples.output: Binary: 1101, bits: pos0=1, pos1=0, pos2=1, pos3=1
examples.trace: Decimal 13. Divide by 2: 13/2=6 rem 1 (bit0=1). 6/2=3 rem 0 (bit1=0). 3/2=1 rem 1 (bit2=1). 1/2=0 rem 1 (bit3=1). So binary 1101 (reading remainders backward). Bits: least significant bit (position 0) is 1, position 1 is 0, position 2 is 1, position 3 is 1.

examples.input: Perform bitwise AND and OR on 6 (binary 110) and 3 (binary 011)
examples.trace: 6 in binary: 110. 3 in binary: 011. Bitwise AND: 110 & 011 = 010 (decimal 2). Bitwise OR: 110 | 011 = 111 (decimal 7). Step-by-step: For AND, bit2: 1&0=0, bit1: 1&1=1, bit0: 0&1=0 → 010. For OR, bit2: 1|0=1, bit1: 1|1=1, bit0: 0|1=1 → 111.

testCases.input: Number of bits needed for 10 elements
Check if bit 1 is set in 6

approaches.name: Approach 3: Optimized DP with Partition States O(n * k * total_sum)
approaches.summary: Employ DP where state dp[i][j] represents the number of ways to partition first i elements into j subsets with a specific sum, using iterative updates to count valid partitions efficiently.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Counting K-Partitions with Equal Sums
ladders.desc: Given an array of integers, count the number of ways to partition it into k subsets with equal sums.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete K-partition counting problem. We need to count all possible ways to divide n elements into k non-empty subsets where each subset has the same sum. The optimized DP approach uses state dp[j][s] representing the number of ways to achieve sum s using j subsets from the processed elements. We process elements one by one, updating states from previous elements. The key insight is that when adding element arr[i], we can either start a new subset with this element or add it to an existing subset, but we must track both the sum and subset count. The algorithm: 1) Calculate total sum, check if divisible by k. 2) Initialize dp[0][0] = 1. 3) For each element, iterate backwards through subset counts and sums to avoid double counting. 4) For each state dp[j][s], we can either add current element to an existing subset (dp[j][s+arr[i]]) or start new subset (dp[j+1][s+arr[i]]). 5) Final answer is dp[k][target] where target = total_sum/k. Time: O(n*k*total_sum) from nested loops. Space: O(k*total_sum) for DP table. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into complete solution. Common mistakes: Not iterating backwards (causes element reuse), forgetting modulo operations for large counts, not handling impossible cases early. Real pattern: Use 2D DP with careful update order.

examples.trace: Total sum = 10, target = 5. Initialize dp[0][0]=1. Process arr[0]=1: dp[1][1]=1 (start new subset). Process arr[1]=2: From dp[1][1]: add to existing -> dp[1][3]=1; start new -> dp[2][2]=1. From dp[0][0]: start new -> dp[1][2]=1. Process arr[2]=3: From dp[2][2]: add to existing -> dp[2][5]=1. From dp[1][3]: add to existing -> dp[1][6]=1; start new -> dp[2][3]=1. From dp[1][2]: add to existing -> dp[1][5]=1; start new -> dp[2][2]=1 (now dp[2][2]=2). Process arr[3]=4: From dp[2][5]: add to existing -> dp[2][9]=1. From dp[2][3]: add to existing -> dp[2][7]=1. From dp[2][2]: add to existing -> dp[2][6]=2. From dp[1][6]: add to existing -> dp[1][10]=1; start new -> dp[2][6]=3 (now 3). From dp[1][5]: add to existing -> dp[1][9]=1; start new -> dp[2][5]=2 (now 2). Final dp[2][5] = 2 ways.

Total sum = 8, target = 2. Initialize dp[0][0]=1. Process arr[0]=2: dp[1][2]=1. Process arr[1]=2: From dp[1][2]: add to existing -> dp[1][4]=1; start new -> dp[2][2]=1. From dp[0][0]: start new -> dp[1][2]=2. Process arr[2]=2: From dp[2][2]: add to existing -> dp[2][4]=1; start new -> dp[3][2]=1. From dp[1][4]: add to existing -> dp[1][6]=1; start new -> dp[2][4]=2. From dp[1][2]: add to existing -> dp[1][4]=2; start new -> dp[2][2]=2. Process arr[3]=2: From dp[3][2]: add to existing -> dp[3][4]=1; start new -> dp[4][2]=1. From dp[2][4]: add to existing -> dp[2][6]=2; start new -> dp[3][4]=2. From dp[2][2]: add to existing -> dp[2][4]=3; start new -> dp[3][2]=2. From dp[1][6]: add to existing -> dp[1][8]=1; start new -> dp[2][6]=3. From dp[1][4]: add to existing -> dp[1][6]=2; start new -> dp[2][4]=5. Final dp[4][2] = 1 way (all subsets {2},{2},{2},{2}).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP State Update for One Element
ladders.desc: Given current DP states and a new element, update all reachable states.
ladders.explanation: This is the core update step in the full algorithm. We have a DP table dp[j][s] representing ways to form j subsets with total sum s using processed elements. When we add a new element val, we need to update all states to include this element. The intuition: For each existing state (j,s), we can either add val to an existing subset (keeping j same, sum becomes s+val) or start a new subset with val (incrementing j to j+1, sum becomes s+val). We must iterate backwards through j and s to avoid reusing the same element multiple times in one update. Algorithm: 1) Create copy of current dp. 2) For j from max_j down to 0, for s from max_sum down to 0: if dp[j][s] > 0: a) Add to existing: new_dp[j][s+val] += dp[j][s] b) Start new: new_dp[j+1][s+val] += dp[j][s]. 3) Return new_dp. Time: O(k*total_sum) per element. Space: O(k*total_sum). Connection: This subroutine is called n times in full solution. Common mistakes: Forward iteration causes element reuse, not handling sum bounds, forgetting to copy states. Real pattern: Nested reverse loops with conditional updates.

examples.trace: Initial dp: dp[0][0]=1, others 0. Copy to new_dp. Iterate j=1 to 0, s=2 to 0. j=1,s=2: dp[1][2]=0 skip. j=1,s=1: 0 skip. j=1,s=0: 0 skip. j=0,s=2: 0 skip. j=0,s=1: 0 skip. j=0,s=0: dp[0][0]=1. Add to existing: new_dp[0][0+2]=new_dp[0][2]+=1 → new_dp[0][2]=1. Start new: new_dp[0+1][0+2]=new_dp[1][2]+=1 → new_dp[1][2]=1. Final new_dp: row0: [1,0,1], row1: [0,0,1], row2: [0,0,0] but max_j=1 so row2 ignored. Actually need 3 sums (0,1,2) so output shows 2x3.

Initial dp: dp[0][1]=1, dp[1][0]=1. Copy. Iterate backwards. j=1,s=2: 0 skip. j=1,s=1: 0 skip. j=1,s=0: dp[1][0]=1. Add to existing: new_dp[1][0+1]=new_dp[1][1]+=1 → new_dp[1][1]=1. Start new: new_dp[2][1] out of bounds (max_j=1) skip. j=0,s=2: 0 skip. j=0,s=1: dp[0][1]=1. Add to existing: new_dp[0][1+1]=new_dp[0][2]+=1 → new_dp[0][2]=1. Start new: new_dp[1][1+1]=new_dp[1][2]+=1 → new_dp[1][2]=1. j=0,s=0: 0 skip. Final new_dp: row0: [0,1,1], row1: [1,1,1] but careful: original dp[1][0]=1 remains in copy so new_dp[1][0]=1. So row0: [0,1,1], row1: [1,1,1].

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP State Representation for Partition Counting
ladders.desc: Define DP state dp[j][s] as number of ways to form j subsets with sum s.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is representing the problem state with two dimensions: number of subsets formed (j) and current total sum of elements in those subsets (s). This captures partial progress toward the final goal of k subsets each with target sum. Intuition: As we process elements sequentially, we need to track both how many subsets we've started and their combined sum, because the final requirement is about per-subset sums. The state evolves by adding elements either to existing subsets or starting new ones. Why this works: It's a form of subset DP where we count combinations rather than just existence. The j dimension ensures we don't exceed k subsets, and s dimension tracks progress toward total sum. Algorithm concept: Initialize dp[0][0]=1 (0 subsets, 0 sum). Transition: dp[j][s] can come from dp[j][s-val] (add to existing) or dp[j-1][s-val] (start new). Time/space: State space is O(k*total_sum). Connection: This state definition enables the update subroutine. Common mistakes: Confusing j with subset sizes, not understanding s is cumulative sum across subsets. Real pattern: 2D array indexed by [subset_count][sum].

examples.input: Process first 2 elements [1,2], target sum per subset=3, k=2
examples.trace: Start: dp[0][0]=1. After element 1: From dp[0][0], start new subset → dp[1][1]=1. After element 2: From dp[1][1]: add to existing → dp[1][3]=1; start new → dp[2][2]=1. From dp[0][0]: start new → dp[1][2]=1. Current dp: dp[1][1]=0? Actually after updates: dp[1][1] was from previous but overwritten? Let's track carefully: After element1: dp[1][1]=1. After element2: Process dp[1][1]=1: add to existing → dp[1][3]=1; start new → dp[2][2]=1. Process dp[0][0]=1: start new → dp[1][2]=1. So final: dp[0][0]=1, dp[1][2]=1, dp[1][3]=1, dp[2][2]=1. Others 0.

Start: dp[0][0]=1. After element 2: dp[1][2]=1. After second element 2: From dp[1][2]=1: add to existing → dp[1][4]=1; start new → dp[2][2]=1. From dp[0][0]=1: start new → dp[1][2]=1 (now total dp[1][2]=1). So dp[1][2]=1, dp[1][4]=1, dp[2][2]=1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Counting Subsets with Given Sum
ladders.desc: Count number of subsets that sum to a target value.
ladders.explanation: This is a simpler problem that builds toward K-partition: count how many subsets of an array have sum exactly equal to target. Intuition: Use DP where dp[s] = number of subsets with sum s. Initialize dp[0]=1 (empty subset). For each element val, update dp from high to low: dp[s] += dp[s-val] for s >= val. This counts all subsets including those with val. Why backwards? To avoid reusing same element multiple times (like in knapsack). Algorithm: 1) Initialize dp array size target+1 with dp[0]=1. 2) For each val in arr: for s from target down to val: dp[s] += dp[s-val]. 3) Return dp[target]. Time: O(n*target). Space: O(target). Connection: This is like the sum dimension of K-partition DP without tracking subset count. Common mistakes: Forward iteration causes counting permutations, not handling large counts. Real pattern: Classic subset sum counting DP.

examples.trace: Initialize dp[0]=1. Process first 2: s=4 to 2: s=4: dp[4]+=dp[2]=0; s=3: dp[3]+=dp[1]=0; s=2: dp[2]+=dp[0]=1 → dp[2]=1. dp: [1,0,1,0,0]. Process second 2: s=4 to 2: s=4: dp[4]+=dp[2]=1 → dp[4]=1; s=3: dp[3]+=dp[1]=0; s=2: dp[2]+=dp[0]=1 → dp[2]=2. dp: [1,0,2,0,1]. Process third 2: s=4 to 2: s=4: dp[4]+=dp[2]=2 → dp[4]=3; s=3: dp[3]+=dp[1]=0; s=2: dp[2]+=dp[0]=1 → dp[2]=3. Final dp[4]=3 (subsets: {2,2} from first two, {2,2} from first and third, {2,2} from second and third).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating DP Arrays Backwards
ladders.desc: Update a DP array in reverse order to avoid reusing elements.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is crucial in many DP problems including subset sum and knapsack. When updating a DP array based on its previous values, iterating backwards ensures each element is used at most once per update. Intuition: If we iterate forwards, when we update dp[s] using dp[s-val], and later update dp[s+something] using the new dp[s], we might be using the same element twice because dp[s] now includes cases with the current element. By going backwards from high to low, we ensure that when we read dp[s-val], it hasn't been updated yet in this iteration, so it represents states without the current element. Algorithm: For s from max_sum down to min_val: dp[s] = operation(dp[s], dp[s-val]). Example: In subset sum counting, dp[s] += dp[s-val]. Time: O(max_sum) per element. Space: O(max_sum). Connection: This pattern is used in the DP update for K-partition. Common mistakes: Wrong bounds, confusing order. Real pattern: for s in range(target, val-1, -1): dp[s] += dp[s-val].

examples.trace: Initial dp[0]=1. Iterate s from 3 down to 2. s=3: dp[3] += dp[3-2]=dp[1]=0 → dp[3]=0. s=2: dp[2] += dp[2-2]=dp[0]=1 → dp[2]=1. s=1 and 0 below val so stop. Final dp = [1,0,1,0].

Initial dp. Iterate s from 3 down to 1. s=3: dp[3] += dp[2]=3 → dp[3]=4+3=7. s=2: dp[2] += dp[1]=2 → dp[2]=3+2=5. s=1: dp[1] += dp[0]=1 → dp[1]=2+1=3. s=0 below val. Wait careful: Actually dp becomes [1,3,5,7]? Let's recalc: Start dp=[1,2,3,4]. s=3: dp[3]=4+dp[2]=4+3=7. s=2: dp[2]=3+dp[1]=3+2=5. s=1: dp[1]=2+dp[0]=2+1=3. So final [1,3,5,7]. But example says [1,3,6,10] which is if forward. So backwards gives [1,3,5,7].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Subset Sum Problem
ladders.desc: Determine if there exists a subset with a given sum.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic prerequisite: given an array of integers, check if any subset sums to a target value. Intuition: Use dynamic programming with a boolean array dp where dp[s] is true if sum s is achievable. Start with dp[0]=true (empty subset). For each element val, update dp from high to low: if dp[s-val] is true, set dp[s]=true. This builds up achievable sums. Why it works: Each element can be either included or excluded, and DP tracks all possible sums. Algorithm: 1) Initialize dp[0]=true, others false. 2) For each val: for s from target down to val: if dp[s-val] then dp[s]=true. 3) Return dp[target]. Time: O(n*target). Space: O(target). Connection: This is the existence version of counting subsets, which extends to K-partition. Common mistakes: Not using backwards iteration, off-by-one errors. Real pattern: Simple DP loop.

examples.trace: Initialize dp[0]=true. Process 1: s=5 to 1: s=5: dp[4] false; s=4: dp[3] false; s=3: dp[2] false; s=2: dp[1] false; s=1: dp[0] true → dp[1]=true. dp: [T,T,F,F,F,F]. Process 2: s=5 to 2: s=5: dp[3] false; s=4: dp[2] false; s=3: dp[1] true → dp[3]=true; s=2: dp[0] true → dp[2]=true. dp: [T,T,T,T,F,F]. Process 3: s=5 to 3: s=5: dp[2] true → dp[5]=true; s=4: dp[1] true → dp[4]=true; s=3: dp[0] true → dp[3]=true (already). Final dp[5]=true.

Initialize dp[0]=true. Process 2: s=5 to 2: s=5: dp[3] false; s=4: dp[2] false; s=3: dp[1] false; s=2: dp[0] true → dp[2]=true. dp: [T,F,T,F,F,F]. Process 4: s=5 to 4: s=5: dp[1] false; s=4: dp[0] true → dp[4]=true. dp: [T,F,T,T,F,F]. Process 6: s=5 to 6? val=6 > target so skip. Final dp[5]=false.
